text: The surgery begins with inspection of the pleural cavity.
We identify the abnormal lung parenchyma, and we can clearly observe the difference between healthy and diseased tissues.
We start by dissecting the pulmonary ligament and addressing some adhesions toward the posterior region, where we find the aberrant artery.
We proceed with the dissection of the artery and encounter a lymph node in this area, which is carefully separated from the vessel.
The artery is then isolated and stapled.
This stapling can be somewhat challenging due to the proximity of the spine, which limits the space for the stapler.
The lymph node was also removed and sent for analysis.
Next, we requested intravenous injection of indocyanin green.
Here, we can clearly see the region of the lung parenchyma that was being perfused by the aberrant artery.
This is the area to be resected.
As planned, we begin marking the lung parenchyma to define the resection margins.
This zone corresponds to the area we initially identified, showing a visible difference from the healthy lung tissue.
At this point, we complete the dissection of the pulmonary ligament to facilitate stapling.
Here we can see the stamp of the stapled artery.
The lung parenchyma is then stapled along the pre-marked line.
At this point, it is crucial to carefully follow the planned resection line to avoid the risk of leaving behind any diseased lung parenchyma.
The specimen is retrieved using an endobag.
